After spending about an hour scrolling each day, I've learned the hard way that you can't trust everything you see. Working as a mechanic for decades taught me one thing: if something doesn't add up, you investigate it. Same principle applies online.

First thing I do is pause before I share anything. If a post makes me angry or excited immediately, I take a step back. That's usually when the fake stuff gets you. I'll check if the source is legitimate by looking at the account's history. Does it have a real profile picture? Are the posts consistent with their supposed expertise? When I see someone claiming to be a doctor but their entire feed is conspiracy theories, I'm skeptical.

I also use reverse image searches, which honestly isn't difficult even for someone like me who isn't super tech-savvy. If a photo seems too dramatic or perfectly framed, I run it through Google Images. You'd be surprised how many "breaking news" images are actually recycled from years ago. I learned this after almost sharing a doctored photo of Naples that turned out to be from 2015.

What really helps is following reliable news sources and fact-checking websites. I've bookmarked a couple Italian fact-checking sites that I trust. When my kids send me something questionable, I check there first before responding. It saves me from looking foolish and keeps me from spreading misinformation to my friends.

The emotional side of this is what gets me most, though. My anxiety makes me vulnerable to fear-mongering posts, so I'm extra careful about health and safety claims. I've learned to ask myself: who benefits from me believing this? Is there a reason someone would want me to share this? response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.

persona.time_onsocialmedia: You spend roughly one hour daily on social media

response_parsed.opinion:
Look, I'll be straight with you—dealing with fake content on social media has become part of my daily routine, and honestly, it gets under my skin. After spending about an hour scrolling each day, I've learned the hard way that you can't trust everything you see. Working as a mechanic for decades taught me one thing: if something doesn't add up, you investigate it. Same principle applies online.

First thing I do is pause before I share anything. If a post makes me angry or excited immediately, I take a step back. That's usually when the fake stuff gets you. I'll check if the source is legitimate by looking at the account's history. Does it have a real profile picture? Are the posts consistent with their supposed expertise? When I see someone claiming to be a doctor but their entire feed is conspiracy theories, I'm skeptical.

I also use reverse image searches, which honestly isn't difficult even for someone like me who isn't super tech-savvy. If a photo seems too dramatic or perfectly framed, I run it through Google Images. You'd be surprised how many "breaking news" images are actually recycled from years ago. I learned this after almost sharing a doctored photo of Naples that turned out to be from 2015.

What really helps is following reliable news sources and fact-checking websites. I've bookmarked a couple Italian fact-checking sites that I trust. When my kids send me something questionable, I check there first before responding. It saves me from looking foolish and keeps me from spreading misinformation to my friends.

The emotional side of this is what gets me most, though. My anxiety makes me vulnerable to fear-mongering posts, so I'm extra careful about health and safety claims. I've learned to ask myself: who benefits from me believing this? Is there a reason someone would want me to share this? That critical thinking has served me well in the garage and serves me just as well online. It's not foolproof, but it's better than blindly trusting everything.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: The persona is a 57-year-old Italian woman with high neuroticism (93) and low agreeableness (28), indicating she's anxious but assertive and skeptical of others. Her mechanical background suggests practical, analytical thinking. Low conscientiousness (30) means she may not follow strict routines but her moderate openness (48) allows her to adapt to new information. Her one hour daily social media use and creative nature inform her perspective. The tone reflects her direct, no-nonsense approach while acknowledging her anxiety-driven caution. Examples draw from her mechanic experience, Italian location, and family responsibilities.
response_parsed.tone: Direct, practical, slightly skeptical and assertive, with underlying anxiety. Honest and straightforward without being overly formal. Uses her mechanic's analytical mindset to explain concepts. Somewhat frustrated but pragmatic.
